We're developing a selective etching process for a GaN-on-Si device structure. The goal is to etch through a 2μm GaN layer and stop on a 50nm AlGaN buffer layer. Using a Cl2/BCl3/Ar chemistry in an ICP reactor, we're achieving good GaN etch rates, but we're experiencing issues with selectivity to the AlGaN layer. How can we optimize this process to maintain high GaN etch rates, achieve good selectivity to AlGaN, and prevent sidewall redeposition?

In our advanced 3D NAND fabrication process, we're encountering challenges with the channel hole etching step. How can we modify our etch strategy to achieve consistent etching through all 128 layers while maintaining the critical dimensions?

How can we optimize this process to achieve anisotropic etching of the entire stack while minimizing undercut of the TiN layer?

We've tried moving to a Cl2/N2 plasma-based dry etch, but we're losing selectivity to the InGaP layer. How can we develop a process that combines the selectivity of the wet etch with the anisotropy of the dry etch to achieve the required dimensional control?
